title: For the Glory of God and the Good of Each Other
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-26-2024 -AM- For the Glory of God and the Good of Each Other - 1 Peter 4_7-11 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The Basis and Goal of Godly Living
subpoints: The basis (indicative): "The end of all things is at hand" — Christ is returning; this is not fairytale but reality
Every generation since the ascension has lived in expectation of His return; the only thing missing now is the full gathering of the elect
Eschatology must cause us to live differently now — studying last things to have no effect on present life misses the purpose
The goal of godly living (verse 11 end): "That in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ"
Everything we do — eating, drinking, parenting, working, thinking, relating — is to be done for the glory of God
If something cannot be done for the glory of God, it must not be done at all

outline.title: God Supplies the Strength to Serve One Another
subpoints: Verse 10: minister as "good stewards of the manifold grace of God"
Verse 11: "let him do it as with the ability which God supplies" — God lavishly provides, not the bare minimum
God does not demand from us what He does not provide for us
How many times does Christ have to die for us to obey Him? Once — all the grace we need is already available

outline.title: Serve One Another Through Prayer
subpoints: "Be serious and watchful in your prayers" — Peter assumes Christians are praying; he describes how to pray
Both words (serious and watchful) relate to praying with a clear, sober mind — don't let your mind wander
Prayer is like oxygen to the spiritual life — the Christian who does not pray dies spiritually
Are we willing to do the hard work of bringing each other before the throne of God consistently and mindfully?

outline.title: Serve One Another Through Fervent Love
subpoints: "Above all things have fervent love for one another" — love is the most important thing you can do for your brothers and sisters
Love is commanded — it is a decision of the will leading to action, not merely an emotion that comes and goes
"Love will cover a multitude of sins" — love does not ignore sin but facilitates forgiveness and overlooking offenses
Where love abounds, small and large offenses are readily overlooked; where love is lacking, every action is viewed with suspicion

outline.title: Serve One Another Through Hospitality
subpoints: "Be hospitable to one another without grumbling" — hospitality is an essential part of the Christian walk
Biblical hospitality is not simply having someone over for dinner — it is investing in people for the sake of furthering the gospel
In the New Testament context: welcoming traveling preachers of the Word into one's home
We give of ourselves and are inconvenienced for the sake of others — and we do it without complaining

outline.title: Serve One Another Through Using Spiritual Gifts
subpoints: Verse 10: "As each one has received a gift, minister it to one another" — everyone in the church has received a gift
The word for gift (charisma) contains the word for grace (charis) — these are not earned but given
The gift is not yours to keep — God gave it to you to manage for His glory, like the parable of the talents
Two examples: speaking (reflecting the elder's office) and serving (reflecting the deacon's office)
Speaking: the pastor and all who teach must speak as the oracles of God — only what God's Word teaches
Serving: when you serve, do it by the ability God supplies — this covers everything from babysitting to finances to visiting the sick
You cannot live a godly life and be self-centered at the same time — these two things cannot coexist

summary:
In this sermon on 1 Peter 4:7-11, Pastor Tito Lyro unfolds Peter's vision for the God-glorifying, other-serving life of the Christian community. He frames the passage with its opening indicative and its closing purpose clause: the basis for everything is that "the end of all things is at hand" — Christ is returning — and the goal of everything is that "in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ." Between these two poles, Peter describes what the Christian life looks like: prayer, love, hospitality, and the exercise of spiritual gifts. These are not optional extras but the very texture of Christian living.

Eschatology, Pastor Lyro argues, must produce godliness now. A study of last things that has no effect on how one lives today misses the purpose for which God gave us prophetic Scripture. Because Christ is coming, we live for His glory — in eating, drinking, parenting, working, thinking, and relating. If something cannot be done for the glory of God, it must not be done at all. And crucially, God does not demand from us what He does not provide — He lavishly supplies the grace to serve, which means the first call is to faithful prayer, drawing on the strength He freely gives.

The four practices Peter commends are all others-oriented. Prayer brings each other before God with a clear, sober mind. Fervent love covers a multitude of sins and keeps a fellowship from becoming a nest of suspicion and conflict. Hospitality is not mere entertaining but a gospel investment — welcoming and supporting those who carry the word. And spiritual gifts — every one of which is a grace-gift, not a wage — are to be managed for God's glory and the building up of others. The two examples Peter gives (speaking and serving) reflect the two offices Christ has given His church: the elder and the deacon. The sermon closes with a pointed challenge: you cannot live a godly life and be self-centered at the same time.
transcript:
## The Basis and Goal of Godly Living

If you're able to remain standing for a moment longer as we read the Word of God, we'll read out of 1 Peter chapter 4, verses 7 through 11.

"But the end of all things is at hand; therefore be serious and watchful in your prayers. And above all things have fervent love for one another, for love will cover a multitude of sins. Be hospitable to one another without grumbling. As each one has received a gift, minister it to one another, as good stewards of the manifold grace of God. If anyone speaks, let him speak as the oracles of God. If anyone ministers, let him do it as with the ability which God supplies, that in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom belong the glory and the dominion forever and ever. Amen."

Let us pray. Father, thank you for your Word. We pray that you speak to us today. We pray that you'll enable me to speak what is true. We pray, Father, that your Spirit will take hold of the things said here today and apply them to our hearts. We pray in Jesus's name. Amen. Please be seated.

In the passage before us today, Peter sets the goal for the life of every Christian. He urges us to live for the glory of God and the good of each other. That's the summary of this passage: live for the glory of God and the good of each other.

I want you to start by seeing that this whole thing is based on an indicative — a statement of truth. The basis for godly living in this passage is that first statement that Peter makes in verse 7: "The end of all things is at hand." Throughout the New Testament, Christ and the apostles based their exhortation to godly living on statements of truth. First, they say: this is what is true. And now this is how you're going to live it out.

The truth that is the basis for the commands that follow is that the end of all things is at hand. Christ is returning. It is a reality — not a fairytale, not a myth, not a legend. It is a reality. Christ is returning. Contrary to those who say that history has no beginning or end, Christ is coming back for the consummation of all things.

Since the ascension of the Lord Jesus Christ, every generation of Christians has lived in the expectation of His return, much like the Old Testament saints lived in the expectation of His first coming. Peter says that is how we must live. In the expectation of the return of the Lord, the only thing Christ is waiting for is the gathering of His church. Every other element of the history of redemption has been accomplished. The only thing missing is the gathering of the elect. And when that happens, the Lord returns. When He does return, all things as we know them will end, and He will establish the new heavens and the new earth.

So we live in the expectation of the return of our Lord to receive us as His bride. We live in the expectation of the resurrection of the last day. We live in the expectation of eternal joy with the Lamb that was slain from the foundation of the world. And we live in the expectation of the last judgment before the Judge of all the earth.

Because of this truth — because Christ is going to come back and judge the living and the dead — Peter exhorts godly living now. Both Paul and John point to the coming of Christ tomorrow as a reason for godly living today. And this must be the emphasis of any study about the things that are going to happen at the end.

For a while the church as a whole has been fixated on figuring out what's going to happen in the last times — from Late Great Planet Earth in the seventies, to 88 Reasons Why Christ Will Return in 1988. The church has often been consumed with that, but in an unhealthy way, where we're only looking to the future and it's having no effect on the here and now. The Bible teaches that what the Bible says is going to happen at the last days must cause us to live life in a godlier way now. We're going to obey Christ today because He's coming tomorrow.

If our study of eschatology — of the things of the last days — makes us forget the here and now, then that study is not fulfilling the purpose for which God designed it. There is no area of theology that is not supposed to be lived out in daily life, and the return of Christ definitely has effects on how we live now.

So Peter gives us not only the basis for godly living — the end of all things is at hand — but also the purpose or goal of all godly living. He does that in verse 11: "That in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ."

We are to live godly lives not because we want to be good boys and girls but because that brings glory to God. The purpose of life is that in all things God be glorified. That is the compass that must guide our lives — not "Is it good for me?" Not "Is it convenient for me?" Not "Is it really what I want?" but "Does this bring glory to God?"

Our lives, brothers and sisters, are to be doxologies to the Lord. Every Lord's Day worship service in the morning we finish with the Doxology, and we ask that all glory be ascribed to the triune God. Well, that is not only Sunday at noon — it is Monday at 3 in the morning, Tuesday at 4, Wednesday at noon. That is what our lives are to be. All of this is to our Lord because what guides us is that all that we do is to be for the glory of the Lord.

John Piper says: "Peter shows that the goal of all of our service as Christians is that God would be glorified as the One who enables all good things." That's our goal in life. Life was created for the glory of God — not just some part of it, but all of life.

As Paul says in 1 Corinthians 10: "Therefore, whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God." Even such mundane things like drinking or eating are to be done for the glory of God. In my eating and in my drinking, I am to glorify God — but also in my relationship with my wife, in my parenting, in my worshiping, in my working, in my thinking. I am to do all those things for the glory of God.

It is not like we can do nine things for the glory of God and then earn the right to do one thing for selfish gain. All things are to always be done for the glory of God. If something cannot be done for the glory of God, then it must not be done at all.

And friends, if you are visiting with us and you are a believer, the only way you can glorify God is through faith in the Lord Jesus Christ. Because apart from Him, you are dead in your trespasses and sins, under the wrath of God, separated from Him. But through Christ, you are brought to life, granted His righteousness. God sees you as perfect. He adopts you as His child and says, "I love him, I love her, because I love my Son Jesus Christ in whom they believe." And from that moment on, you are able to glorify God in Jesus Christ.

## God Supplies the Strength to Serve One Another

Between the indicative — "the end of all things is at hand" — and the goal — "all is for the glory of God" — Peter helps us understand what a godly life looks like. And Peter says: the goal of the godly life is lived by promoting each other's good.

So, "Pastor, I don't want to bless people. I want to live my own life. I want to do the things that I want to do." That's too bad. Because what God wants us to do is to live for the sake of others. Peter puts godly living in terms of four practices that are all others-oriented: prayer, love, hospitality, and exercising spiritual gifts.

Now notice first that it is God who supplies the strength and the grace to obey Him. Look at the end of verse 10: "Minister to one another as good stewards of the manifold grace of God." You're able to serve one another because God provides you the grace. And verse 11: "Let him do it as with the ability which God supplies."

God does not demand from us what He does not provide for us. He is not saying: go do what you can't do. He is saying: go do what I have already given you the grace to do. Go live for others.

As Piper says: "God's strength to serve is a gift. God supplies it. When we believe that and lean on it consciously, we show God to be the glorious giver, and the giver gets the glory." And the word "supplies" in our translation means lavishly provides. God is not giving you the least possible amount of grace you need. He lavishly provides the grace to serve. There is no skimping in God's provision of grace that enables us to serve Him and to serve one another.

Brothers and sisters, how many times does Christ have to die for us to obey Him? Just once — and He has done that already. All the grace that we need is available to us already. You may say, "I don't feel like doing it" — and that's fine, because God is not asking you to feel it. He's just asking you to do it.

## Serve One Another Through Prayer

So Peter says, we serve one another — how? By prayer. Verse 7: "Be serious and watchful in your prayers."

Notice this is not really a command to pray. It just assumes that you as a follower of Jesus Christ are praying. There is no such thing in biblical categories as a Christian who doesn't pray, because prayer is like oxygen to the life of a believer. You don't pray, you die spiritually. So Peter assumes you as a Christian are praying, and so he tells us how he describes how we should be praying.

Both words he uses — "serious" and "watchful" — relate to the idea of praying with a clear mind. The first means that we are to pray thoughtfully, using our minds. The second is the word for sober — not drunk — and here has the idea of not letting your mind get cluttered when you pray. Both of these words are exhortations to take prayer seriously.

So brothers and sisters, are we praying for one another? Are we willing to do the hard work of bringing each other before the throne of God in a consistent, concentrated, mindful way? That is the best way for us to serve one another. As John Bunyan often said: "You can pray, you can do more than pray after you have prayed, but you cannot do more than pray until you have prayed."

## Serve One Another Through Fervent Love

And in verse 8: "And above all things have fervent love for one another, for love will cover a multitude of sins."

Peter says that if anything else, the best you can do for your brothers and sisters is to love them. Early on in chapter 1 verse 22, Peter already told us: love one another fervently with a pure heart.

And notice that it is a command to love. We live in a society and culture that says love is more like a hole — like a hole in the ground that you fall into and fall out of. The Bible says that love is something we decide to do. This is a command. We're told to love one another. A commentator says: love is capable of being commanded because it is not primarily an emotion but a decision of the will leading to action.

And notice that the command is not just to love but to love with a fervent, deep, earnest love. Why is love so important in the church of Jesus Christ? Peter says: because it covers a multitude of sins. Love doesn't ignore sin. Love doesn't pretend there isn't sin. But love deals with sin. Love forgives sin. It facilitates forgiveness.

As one commentator says: "Where love abounds in a fellowship of Christians, many small offenses, and even some large ones, are readily overlooked and forgotten. But where love is lacking, everywhere is viewed with suspicion. Every action is liable to misunderstanding, and conflicts abound to Satan's perverse delight."

But as the apostle John says: we love because He first loved us. The ability to love with this kind of love comes from having a heart changed by the Holy Spirit and a life of living faith in Jesus Christ.

## Serve One Another Through Hospitality

And then in verse 9: "Be hospitable to one another without grumbling."

We know what grumbling is, right? Complaining, murmuring. This expectation to be hospitable seems to be out of place. We don't often think of hospitality as something essential to our Christian walk. But Peter says it is. And the problem is with our thinking, not with Peter — because Peter is inspired by the Holy Spirit, and what he says is right and proper.

Hospitality is here because in being hospitable we are giving of ourselves to others. We are being inconvenienced for the sake of others. And we are to do it without complaining.

And this hospitality is not simply having somebody over for dinner — though it includes that. It is the investment into people for the sake of furthering the gospel. That's what biblical hospitality is. We are investing in people with the purpose of furthering the gospel. What we see often in the New Testament is the idea of welcoming into a house a stranger you never met because that stranger is traveling around preaching the Word of God. Hospitality with the goal of furthering the gospel — and we are to do that as we serve one another.

## Serve One Another Through Using Spiritual Gifts

And fourthly, in verses 10-11, Peter says: use what God has given you to serve others.

"As each one has received a gift, minister it to one another as good stewards of the manifold grace of God. If anyone speaks, let him speak as the oracles of God. If anyone ministers, let him do it as with the ability which God supplies."

By saying this, Peter implies that everyone in the church has received a gift — a talent, a skill, a capability that can and must be used for the good of others and of the church. And whatever that gift is, you are to use it in serving others.

The word "minister" here is the word for service — the same root as our word "deacon." And the reason you are to use your gift for others is because it does not belong to you. It was given to you by the Lord. It is God's, and you are given that gift to manage it for His glory. The word for gift is charisma — which includes the word for grace, charis. These gifts are not wages; they are given regardless of our deserving, and that is why Peter adds that they are given to us according to the manifold grace of God.

Then Peter gives us two examples. They are not the only gifts in the New Testament, but they give us two examples of gifts that can be used for the service of everyone. They reflect the two offices given by Christ to the church: speaking (the office of elder) and serving (the office of deacon).

How does the gift of speaking work in the church? Applied to the pastor: as he has been recognized by the church as having the gift of speaking or teaching, he is to speak as the oracles of God. That means he is only to speak and teach what the Word of God speaks and teaches. It doesn't mean his words are inspired or infallible like the Bible — but it does mean that every time he gets up to preach or to teach, he must be bringing God's word, not his own. And that's what you should demand of me: sermons and lessons that are thoroughly biblical, not just filled with stories.

But the gift of speaking also applies to anyone who teaches at the church — Sunday School teachers, youth group leaders, children's ministry workers, ladies' fellowship teachers. Anyone who stands to teach about God or how one must live must do so according to and with the Word of God. Nobody can say "you ought to do something" if that's not backed up by the Word of God.

And it applies to everyone in the church. This is a gift we all share in some measure. As Paul says in Colossians 3:16: "Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly" — and then he says how that's done: by speaking to each other in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs. The Word of God dwells richly in us when we pour it into others. If you are a black hole of God's Word — a consumer of God's Word that is not transforming how you speak to other people — then the Word of God is not dwelling richly in you.

He lists the gift of serving as well. "If anyone ministers, let him do it as with the ability which God supplies." The word "if" does not indicate conditionality. It's a simple factual conditional — the same strength as "when." When you serve, serve this way. God expects all of us to serve, not just some. And the type of service is characterized by the diaconal office: helping the physical needs of the church, the poor, the widow, the sick, the one moving, whatever it is that can be done to bless people.

So Peter places before us the fact that a godly life is marked by serving others. You cannot live a godly life and be self-centered at the same time. Those two things cannot coexist. Either you are living a godly life or you are self-centered. Brother and sister, if you are the center of your universe, you are not living a godly life. And if you cannot think of one person in your life who is the center of their universe, it might be because it's you.

God calls us to serve others. He lavishly provides the ability to all who serve. We have the ability. We have the grace. We have the strength to do so. We just need to go out and do it.

So Peter sets out the goal for life. He urges us to live for the glory of God and the good of each other. Now it's up to us, by the grace of God, to go out and live for His glory and for the good of others — so that we can say with Peter: to Jesus Christ belong all glory and all dominion over the church and over our hearts.

Let us pray together. God in heaven, thank you that You are a God who is gracious and that in Jesus Christ has bestowed all gifts upon us. We thank You that the ascended Christ has equipped the church to do all that You call us to do. Help us to take hold of Your grace and to live lives that are lives of service, that we might be looking for the coming of Jesus Christ and that Your glory will be our guide in what we do. We ask in Jesus's name. Amen.
